author: @rohan112345

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's a question of thickness. I made a very thin coat because it is spun cast. Others are using drop casting and that leaves a thicker coat. If I made it thicker it would be black.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: This is why you wash it in HCl and DI water. Plus nothing wrong with being skeptic but this method isn't exotic I'm afraid. It's a fairly standard way to make GO. It is only a modified Hummer's method. If you are not very experienced don't be discouraged if your first attempt doesn't work - try again you will get there

Does this mean that the sample of graphite oxide is not pure then? Eagerly expecting your reply.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The problem with nanoscale stuff is that size matters. The properties of materials change when moved down to the nanoscale. If you want a nanoscale material - you have a challenge. If you want a material that is just 'thin' lots of things will do. Laquers, paints, varnishes, thin film deposits and thick film deposite - just loads of things. You could try making your own paint of TiO2.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: As far as I know Chitosan isn't soluable in water. It is soluable in a weak acid But not water. It kind of goes like a jelly - it absobs the water certainly - but then that would help with it's role as a solid electrolyte. Anyhow, If your requirements are particularly stringent i would have though anything would do. Varnish for instance - though that is mesoporous. I think I am not really seeing your problem. Ah, I had an epiphany while writing this.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: please do. And no probs mate - always happy to help

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: you could then attach a plastic film to the graphene side of the copper sheet and etch away the copper with ferric chloride to get a graphene coated plastic sheet - I am not sure if this would work - but I like the theory and it certainly something to experiment on if you want to give it a go. - it just goes to show - good ideas come from anywhere - so keep on thinking my friend.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: on whatever substrate you put it on. You could put it on glass slip and vary the concentration of sugar and acid until you get a monolayer. The cover that in copper foil. Heat the copper foil - i'll check the temperature but its around 600 i think - not too difficult a toaster gets hotter - The carbon should be absorbed into the copper. Then you let the copper cool. The carbon will come out of the copper but it will come out as an ordered graphene sheet.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: No worries mate, life is just one big learning experience and i was thinking about your idea a bit - witth a little modification i think it might well work. If you add sugar and sulphuric acid together the sulphuric acid will carbonise the sugar - it does this by dehydrating it down to the atomic level. If you make a sugar weak acid solution and dry it out it will dry to carbon. Now, if you make the sugar solution of the right strength it should form a monolayer of sugar.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: the reason i think this is because things happen differently at the nano scale and that method tiesin a little with an experiment i am currently running to make graphite by coating sand in a monolayer of sucrose with sulphuric acid. I have carbonised the sugar and I think graphetised it - i now just have to get the graphite off as graphene sheets - if that works i will be posting a video on it. But a similar method might be to coat copper nanoparticles and flash it like you suggest. Interesting.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I suspect not - but to be honest - I don't really know. I've never tried it - I suspect it won't work - but that doesn't mean it won't work - it just means I think it won't - one of the best ways to answer questions like this is to try it and see. Why do you think the method you are proposing might work? One thing that might work is to coat the glass with copper nanoparticles, then oil, then pyrolise it (I think you are using sulphuric acid and voltage to pyrolise?), then camera flash.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: haven't bought it yet - I thought I sent you my email - did you get it? In case you didn't - it's robertmurraysmith64@gmail.com - drop me a line about your collaboration ideas

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: No - sorry mate - no chance - at best you will make an amorphous carbon - Usually graphetisation requires high heat - if you are looking at feedstocks like oils you might want to consider chemical vapour deposition with a copper foil substrate - that would work but only because the copper at high heat absorbs the carbon the releases it as it cools. It releases it in an ordered way and a grahene sheet is formed. But what you are suggesting wouldn't work. sorry

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The nitrate is there as an oxidiser, the sulphuric acid is an intercalation agent. Basically the oxidiser lifts the edge of the graphene to allow intercalation to happen. So, I haven't tried aqua regia as I don't think it will do much. But, if you try it and it works let me know will you? Cheers

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hi Eric I like some of the points you raise. So, I have put the graphite away for the moment until I get a sonic actor so no tests as yet. However, I have been playing with graphemes intercalated compounds with some very interesting results. I haven't really looked at caps my main focus is for conductive inks but I am happy to try and help if you want to send more details - but do so by email not on the general board cheers

author: @ericgoeken
text: Hi Robert, I was wondering how the GO turned out.  Were you able to disperse it in water?  My interest lies in using it for graphene supercaps.  Have you tried to reduce it to graphene using a lightscribe?  Also, I have been researching electrolytes and separators to use.  Some are unavailable or expensive to the general public.  I have also been trying to figure out an architecture to house the entire cell.  I know this is a lot, but have you had any thoughts or insights into these areas?

author: @YFUATIW
text: Have you tried aqua regia for initial dissolution?  A combination of nitric and hydrochloric.  In your first vid you added sodium nitrate to sulfuric, which should result in a nitric solution to start with.  Just curious.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i have read of researchers using an ultrasonic cleaning bath so i think it will do the job - a lab sonicator is a robe type that concentrates the power in a probe type tip called a sonotrode - it basically acts as an amplifier and concentrates the action in a much smaller area and so it it more powerful - that's about the whole difference

author: @eat808
text:
Do you have a 3D printer or are you interested in 3D printing technology, a guy in Glasgow university has modified one to do chemical reactions automatically via printed "reaction-ware" chambers, I am looking into the possibility of modifying a reprap derivative into a bench-top chemical factory for the automatic manufacture of graphene solar cells, essentially 3D printed solar cells.

Sorry for the triple posting!

author: @eat808
text:

I ask as I already have a jewelers ultrasonic bath (albeit broken!) that I could fix up for sonicating the GO before experimenting with Laser-scribing, I am primarily interested in graphene for the production of supercapacitors/batteries and solar cells.

text:

Thanks Robert for sharing your expertise I am truly amazed at the progress you've made with replicating the GO manufacturing process at home, your part 2 video shows that you have manged to find a simpler process that seems to yield a far purer GO product (hence the yellow colour)

author: @ericgoeken
text: Thanks sounds easy enough.  I was actually thinking about dispersion as well.  Lab sonicators are well out of the price range.  I was thinking about using a jewelry or eyeglass cleaner.  There is actually a good article on a diy sonicator on MAKE website written by becky stern.  Not sure if it would work but the device will only set you back $20.  Otherwise I have also thought about maybe using one of those sonic toothbrushes as well.  Might be another cheap option for dispersion.  Good luck!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The washing was pretty easy - just chuck it into 7 litres of warm water - let it settle - pour off the water and repeat twice more - the pH goes to 4 to 5 and you are done. I have mine sitting here in a jar but I am now having a problem dispesing it - really i need a sonicator - not sure what to do about that at the moment

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: yep - that's it. The initial reaction was almost immediate - my guess is - if you are happy with a lower yield you wouldn't enev have to let it sitting for 3 days - 1 day would probably be good enough for an 80% yield. I haven't tried it though - so it's only a guess

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
sorry for that - spelling mistake - 1 molar solution. to make a one molar from 37.5% stock HCL (which I what I used)1M HCl Solution
Concentrated HCl. its 37.5%.
The density of concentrated HCl is 1.189g/ml we will need this number as well, and of course the atomic mass of HCl 36.46
Calculation=((37.5/100)(1000)(1.189))/36.46 = 12.2M
If HCl concentrated is 12.2 M then to make a 1L solution of 1M HCL

(12.2) x = 1(1)

x = 1(1)/(12.2)
=0.082ml/ml of water
= 82mL HCl per liter

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i know - the price of this stuff is what set me off - Not only the price of the material but the price of the research papers - I can't tell you how long i spend trawling the net for info! - Kaner's stuff is good and he has a couple of videos out showing how he does it too. The sulphuric/phosphoric method i used is pretty impressive thought - one of the best i have tried and in the past couple of months i have tried a few! - so i can recommend it.

author: @ericgoeken
text: I actually started with Richard Kaner's paper on using a laserscribe to reduce the graphite oxide (GO) film to graphene.  It describes the process pretty thoroughly.  However, for the GO, it references a paper on using a modified hummers method to synthesize the GO.  But I cant find that paper for free.  Otherwise, I have found sources to buy powdered GO, but at like $160 for 500mg which would only make about a half cup of suspended GO in water.

author: @RensFilius
text: How interesting Robert. We are tinking of relicating this for a schoolproject. One thing was not clear. What do you mean by 'wash this in 1 moar HCl'? What does 'moar' mean?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: cheers mate! - i try lol.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: it's a bit of a combination of Huang et al and something i read in carbon XXX - the huang paper is from the international journal of nanomedicine - simple room temperature preparation of high yield large area graphene oxide. I can't remember the other paper - but i do remember it said to quench the reaction in a large volume of hot water - and that seems to work pretty well when i used it. Good luck with your replication - i am finding the washing process very tedious by the way - any help?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: wow - deepest respect - you are bang on! - i'm pretty impressed to be honest. I though of using a fresnel lens to concentrate the solar and then relying on graphene's thermal conductivity to take this into the tube - what do you think? any good?

author: @Canadianhonkindiesel
text: Correct me if I am wrong but isn't the reijke tube based on using heat applied to a steel mesh within a glass tube to create a sound resonant frequency? Perhaps you just mean to use a glass tube with a conductive fine mesh(+), a coating of the graphine & back conductive layer (-). In combination with a solar concentrator. Keep us posted ... curious!

I'm very interested to see your next videos on the process as well.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: what's the substrate? - if it was me and 10x10 was fixed i'd probably spin coat it onto PET and microwave reduce. If you don't mind it being a circle of 12cm diameter - a good way is to coat it onto a PET disc and spin it up in a lightscribe. That's pretty much what I am going to do once i have finished washing the oxide and give it a go at making a suprcapacitor.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: cheers stefan i'll try it

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's a good demonstration - i like it and yes it reminds me of the carbon snake - as i am sure you know sugar and sulphuric acid. However, when you exfoliate intercalated graphene compounds in a microwave you get pretty much the same effect. My guess would be that it is an intercalated compound and what ever he pours on it is a catalyst for an exfoliation heat based reaction. I did look at this for a while but the intercalated compounds are fairly exotic and difficult to make.
